sections.heading: Intro
sections.content:
What? This is a subdomain tools review. Originally written in December 2020, this page has been updated with current tool status and new additions as of 2025.
Why? Sometimes I have doubts if I am actually finding all the subdomains when I start hunting and if the tool I use will find them all. This is the review that I would like to have read before deciding on one tool or another.
How? As the main objective is to find subdomains, I have compared tools by their approach:
 Passive: It relies on third-party services with which it collects the largest possible number of subdomains, dead or alive. The problem with this approach is that you can find numerous subdomains, but many of them may be prehistoric, but in return they do it very quickly.
 Active: From any source, for example third-party sources of the passive approach, it verifies through DNS requests (or in any other way) if the subdomain is alive or not. This approach takes a little longer than the passive one, but the results it generates are almost entirely useful.
 Bruteforce: From a wordlist and a domain, it makes DNS requests for each word along with the domain. The advantage of this approach is that the results obtained are always real, but it depends entirely on the quality of the wordlist.
 Alterations/permutations: In this case, from a list of subdomains and a list of alterations or permutations, a new list of subdomains is generated that are verified through DNS requests. With this approach you can find subdomains that with the rest would be impossible.
Recommended resources:
 Resolvers: trickest/resolvers
 Bruteforce: n0kovosubdomains or assetnote wordlists
* Alterations: altdns/words.txt
{% hint style="info" %}
This is not intended to be a serious investigation, a technical paper, or anything like that, just a series of tests that I have done for fun. The results shown are my opinion and if at any time you don't like them, or you don't agree, you can stop reading or explain to me how I could have done it better 😉
{% endhint %}
Original 2020 test results: Google Sheets
{% embed url="https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1Fadv4jnMCDcpa_RQy12TpEZQNo1l8KsDFJscPjnePo/edit?usp=sharing" %}

rows.Notes: subfinder is faster, amass more thorough
puredns for large lists with massdns backend
Fast DNS toolkit, replaced many tools

sections.heading: Tools
sections.content: Small summary of each tool with the features and results that I got. This section not follows any special order.

subheadings.heading: Pros
content: Swiss army knife for subdomains enumeration, all the functionalities you can think of and more.
It added active subdomains that none of the other tools managed to add.

subheadings.heading: Cons
content: Sometimes usability is confusing due to the large number of options

subheadings.heading: Cons
content: Limited features, such as bruteforce without the ability to specify a custom wordlist.

subheadings.heading: Pros
content: Consults in one of the best data sources.

subheadings.heading: Cons
content: Nothing in particular, does a very specific thing and does it well.

subheadings.heading: Pros
content: Allow to update dataset with your own findings.

subheadings.heading: Pros
content: Fast compared with others with similar number of integrations.
Lot of options for search, filters and output.

subheadings.heading: Cons
content: Amass got a few subdomains that subfinder missed only in the large scope.

subheadings.heading: Cons
content: In some cases, it missed some subdomains that the rest did.

subheadings.heading: Pros
content: Really fast for the amount of services integrated.

subheadings.heading: Cons
content: No results not found by others.

subheadings.heading: Cons
content: Not subdomains feature, you have to filter with some tool like unfurl or grep.

subheadings.heading: Pros
content: GitHub is always a useful source.

subheadings.heading: Cons
content: With some common names or companies could be very slow.

subheadings.heading: Pros
content: Option to show CNAME or IP.

subheadings.heading: Pros
content: Lot of DNS options to optimize.

subheadings.heading: Pros
content: The IP, CDN and CIDR support

subheadings.heading: Cons
content: In some cases, it missed some subdomains that the rest did.

subheadings.heading: Pros
content: Multiple options apart the subdomain search.

subheadings.heading: Cons
content: No results not found by others.
Active scans output could be better.

subheadings.heading: Pros
content: Free version is still completely useful.

subheadings.heading: Cons
content: Paid version has all the features.
No customizable output file in free version.

subheadings.heading: Passive
subheadings.content:
With amass and subfinder this part is more than completed, but there are other tools that, depending on the objective, may provide valuable information.
1. subfinder
2. amass
3. Findomain
4. Sudomy
5. sublist3r

subheadings.heading: Active
subheadings.content:
In this field subfinder is the best, I find it to get results incredibly fast.
1. Findomain
2. subfinder
3. Sudomy
4. Amass

subheadings.heading: Bruteforce
subheadings.content:
Again projectdiscovery does a great job with shuffledns and is far from the rest of the tools in speed and options.
1. shuffledns
2. Findomain
3. dnscan
4. gobuster
5. aiodnsbrute

subheadings.heading: Alterations
subheadings.content:
I don't find alterations and permutations with resolution useful, but in case you like it, dmut should be your option by far.
1. dmut
2. altdns

sections.heading: Final thoughts
sections.content: When I started the review, I believed that amass would be the winner in most cases, but it seems that I have found new tools with which to improve the workflow.

sections.heading: 2025 Update
sections.content:
The landscape has evolved significantly since 2020:
What's Changed:
- projectdiscovery tools have become the de facto standard (subfinder, dnsx, puredns ecosystem)
- puredns has replaced shuffledns for most use cases due to better accuracy
- alterx is the modern replacement for altdns with better performance
- Chaos database is now public (no invite needed)
- crt.sh rate limiting has increased, many tools now cache results
Current Recommended Workflow:
``bash
# 1. Passive enumeration
subfinder -d target.com -all -o subspassive.txt
# 2. Bruteforce (optional, time-consuming)
puredns bruteforce wordlist.txt target.com -r resolvers.txt -w subsbrute.txt
# 3. Combine and resolve
cat subspassive.txt subsbrute.txt | sort -u | puredns resolve -r resolvers.txt -w subsresolved.txt
# 4. Permutations on resolved (optional)
cat subsresolved.txt | alterx | puredns resolve -r resolvers.txt -w subs_final.txt
`
Or use an automated pipeline:
`bash
# reconftw handles all of this and more
reconftw -d target.com -r
``
Thanks to all the tools developers who facilitate our work and implement the recon methodology better and better.
